papers.title: Anti-distillation: Improving reproducibility of deep networks
papers.abstract: Deep networks have been revolutionary in improving performance of machine learning and artificial intelligence systems. Their high prediction accuracy, however, comes at a price of model irreproducibility in very high levels that do not occur with classical linear models. Two models, even if supposedly identical—with identical architecture, identical trained parameter sets, and trained on the same examples—may predict very differently on individual, previously unseen, examples. Prediction differences may be as large as the order of magnitude of the predictions themselves. Ensembles have been shown to somewhat mitigate this behavior, but may not be utilizing their full potential. In this work, Anti-Distillation is proposed to address irreproducibility in deep networks: ensemble components are forced away from one another by de-correlating their outputs over mini-batches, making them more diverse and the final predictions more reproducible. Empirical results demonstrate substantial prediction-difference reductions on benchmark and real datasets.

papers.title: Smooth activations and reproducibility in deep networks
papers.authors: Gil I. Shamir, Dong Lin, Lorenzo Coviello
papers.abstract: Deep networks are gradually penetrating almost every domain in our lives due to their amazing success. However, with substantive performance accuracy improvements comes the price of irreproducibility: two identical models trained on the exact same dataset may exhibit large differences in predictions on individual examples, especially when trained on highly distributed parallel systems. The popular Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation has been key to recent success of deep networks, but ReLU is also a catalyzer to irreproducibility. We show that activations smoother than ReLU can provide not only better accuracy but also better accuracy-reproducibility tradeoffs. We propose Smooth ReLU (SmeLU), a new family of activations designed to give such better tradeoffs while keeping the mathematical expression simple and implementation cheap. SmeLU is monotonic, mimics ReLU, and provides continuous gradients, yielding better reproducibility. We further show that SmeLU and its generalizations are special cases of a more general methodology of REctified Smooth Continuous Unit (RESCU) activations. Empirical results demonstrate the superior accuracy-reproducibility tradeoffs with smooth activations, SmeLU in particular.

papers.title: A large-scale field experiment to evaluate the effectiveness of paid search advertising
papers.authors: Lorenzo Coviello, Uri Gneezy, Lorenz Goette
papers.abstract: Companies spend billions of dollars online for paid links to branded search terms. Measuring the effectiveness of this marketing spending is hard. Blake, Nosko and Tadelis (2015) ran an experiment with eBay, showing that when the company suspended paid search, most of the traffic still ended up on its website. Can findings from one of the largest companies in the world be generalized? We conducted a similar experiment with Edmunds.com, arguably a more representative company, and found starkly different results. More than half of the paid traffic is lost when we shut off paid-links search. These results suggest money spent on search-engine marketing may be more effective than previously documented.

papers.title: Detecting Emotional Contagion in Massive Social Networks
papers.authors: Lorenzo Coviello, Yunkyu Sohn, Adam D.I. Kramer, Cameron Marlow, Massimo Franceschetti, Nicholas A. Christakis, James H. Fowler
papers.abstract: We detect the presence of massive-scale emotional contagion via social networks by examining naturally occurring data. Using rainfall as a natural experiment—since rain induces negative affect in those who experience it—we show that people exposed to more positive emotional content in their social feed subsequently post more positive content themselves, and vice versa. Using data from over one billion Facebook users, we find that emotional states can be transferred to others via social network ties, leading people to express similar emotions without direct interaction or awareness. These results demonstrate that the emotional content diffusing through large-scale networks can shape emotional expression on a global scale.

papers.title: Finding Red Balloons with Split Contracts: Robustness to Individuals' Selfishness
papers.authors: Manuel Cebrian, Lorenzo Coviello, Andrea Vattani, Panagiotis Voulgaris
papers.abstract: The present work deals with the problem of information acquisition in a strategic networked environment. To study this problem, Kleinberg and Raghavan (FOCS 2005) introduced the model of query incentive networks, where the root of a binomial branching process wishes to retrieve an information known by each node independently with probability 1/n by investing as little as possible. Kleinberg and Raghavan discovered that the investment needed at the root exhibits an unexpected threshold behavior that depends on the branching parameter b. For b>2, the investment is linear in the expected distance to the closest information (logarithmic in n), while for 1<b<2 it becomes exponential. Motivated by the empirical success of the MIT Media Lab team in the DARPA Network Challenge—who won by implementing a split-contract incentive scheme rather than fixed payments—we present a comprehensive theoretical study of split contracts in the game-theoretic setting of query incentive networks. Our main result is that split contracts are robust to nodes' selfishness: the threshold behavior observed in prior work vanishes, and an investment linear in the expected distance to the closest information suffices to retrieve it in any Galton-Watson process with b>1.
